did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning object detection models, specifically benchmarking 32 architectures from the YOLO family (v5–v12) and RT-DETR, to perform skin lesion detection and classification on a novel dataset including healthy skin images. The study identified YOLOv9c as the superior model for this dermatological task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the curation of a novel dataset of 10,000 images and a comprehensive comparative benchmark of 32 object detection models (YOLOv5–v12 and RT-DETR). It details specific methods, multi-stage evaluation processes, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., YOLOv9c achieving 72.5% mAP@50), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enchancing Robustness in Skin Lesion Detection: A Benchmark of 32 Models on a Novel Dataset Including Healthy Skin Images
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for robust skin lesion detection by benchmarking 32 object detection models, primarily from the YOLOv5–v12 and RT-DETR families, on a novel dataset curated from the ISIC archive. The dataset comprises 10,000 images covering 10 distinct lesion classes (benign and malignant) and includes a dedicated background class of 1,000 healthy skin images to mitigate false positives. Models were evaluated using a multi-stage process considering performance metrics like mAP and Recall, as well as computational complexity. The YOLOv9c model emerged as the superior architecture, achieving a mAP@50 of 72.5% and a Recall of 71.6%. The research establishes a new benchmark for dermatological diagnostics and highlights the critical importance of including healthy skin images to enhance clinical viability.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; achieved highest performance metrics (Recall: 95.3%, mAP@50: 95.8%).
Benign lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; strong performance (Recall: 90%, mAP@50: 93.3%).
Benign lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; augmented from 711 to 1000 images.
Benign lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; challenging within benign group (mAP@50: 64.2%).
Benign lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; strong performance (Recall: 89.4%, mAP@50: 93%).
Malignant lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; detected in 63 cases correctly.
Malignant lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; poor detection rate (Recall: 51%); visually similar to AK.
Malignant lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; most problematic class (Recall: 48.5%, mAP@50: 44.4%); blurred, barely visible spots.
Malignant lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; highest recall among malignant classes (77.0%).
Malignant lesion class; 1000 images in the curated dataset; detected in 63 cases correctly.
Background class; 1000 images of clear skin without lesions; critical for mitigating false positives and distinguishing presence/absence of lesions.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for 10 distinct skin lesion classes and a background class (healthy skin).
all_task_types.details: The paper benchmarks 32 object detection models (YOLOv5–v12, RT-DETR) for skin lesion detection. The task involves localizing lesions via bounding boxes and classifying them into discrete categories (benign/malignant lesions and healthy skin background). Performance is evaluated using object detection metrics such as mAP@50, mAP@50-95, Recall, and Precision. The study explicitly contrasts its detection approach with previous classification-only studies.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Custom Dataset
datasets_used.description: A novel, balanced dataset curated by the authors from the public ISIC archive. It includes 10 distinct skin lesion classes (benign and malignant) and a dedicated background class of healthy skin images. Background images were obtained by cropping healthy skin fragments from high-resolution ISIC images. The dataset was augmented to balance class sizes and split into training, validation, and testing subsets.

use.training: Used to train 32 object detection models (YOLOv5–v12, RT-DETR) in a multi-stage evaluation process.
use.validation: Used for model selection and filtering during the multi-stage evaluation process.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the selected YOLOv9c model and compare it with other studies.
use.other: The dataset was made publicly available on Zenodo to foster further research.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study used a deterministic multi-stage evaluation and scoring system based on performance metrics (mAP, Recall) and computational complexity to select the best model.

ISIC Custom Dataset (Test Set).Notes: The YOLOv9c model was selected as the superior architecture. It achieved the highest score in a multi-stage evaluation system combining performance metrics and computational complexity. The inclusion of a background class (healthy skin) was critical for mitigating false positives. The model performed best on benign lesions (especially PBK) and worst on malignant lesions (especially AK). The optimal training duration was determined to be 75 epochs. At a confidence threshold of 0.340, the F1 score was 0.69.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the COCO dataset (80 object categories) with transfer learning applied for fine-tuning on 10 skin lesion classes.

architecture_details.family: YOLO (You Only Look Once) v5–v12 and RT-DETR
architecture_details.selected_architecture_features: YOLOv9 utilizes Programmable Gradient Information (PGI) and a GELAN backbone to prevent information loss in deep networks. The 'c' variant stands for Compact/Conventional, equivalent to the 'Large' size in other YOLO versions.
architecture_details.backbone: GELAN backbone (for YOLOv9); CSPDarknet53 (for YOLOv5); ResNet (for RT-DETR)
architecture_details.heads: Anchor-free detection with a decoupled head (YOLOv8+); Dense predictions with NMS post-processing (YOLOv5-v7); End-to-end set prediction with Hungarian matching (RT-DETR)

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA DGX A100 (8x NVIDIA A100 SXM4 80 GB Tensor Core)

classification_or_output_head.type: Bounding box regression and class classification

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported for YOLOv9c in the text, but the selection criterion required models to have a parameter count not exceeding 30 million. YOLOv9c is described as the 'Compact' or 'Conventional' variant, equivalent to the 'Large' version in other YOLO architectures.

preprocessing_techniques: Bounding box annotation using Roboflow computer vision tools
Cropping of healthy skin fragments from high-resolution ISIC images to create background class
Image augmentation using Albumentations 1.3.1 library (SafeRotate and ShiftScaleRotate) to balance class sizes
Dataset splitting into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) subsets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied using Albumentations 1.3.1 library to augment the dermatofibroma and solar lentigo classes to balance class sizes (increasing counts to 1000 images each).
Applied using Albumentations 1.3.1 library to augment the dermatofibroma and solar lentigo classes to balance class sizes (increasing counts to 1000 images each).

conclusions: The YOLOv9c model was identified as the superior architecture for skin lesion detection among 32 tested models, achieving an mAP@50 of 72.5% and a Recall of 71.6%.
Including a dedicated 'background' class of healthy skin images is a critical step for mitigating false positives and creating clinically viable diagnostic tools.
The study established a new benchmark for automated dermatological diagnosis using a novel, balanced dataset of 10,000 images comprising 10 lesion classes and a background class.

Model performance varied significantly by lesion type, with the YOLOv9c model demonstrating strong effectiveness on benign lesions (e.g., Pigmented Benign Keratosis with 95.8% mAP@50) but lower performance on malignant lesions, particularly Actinic Keratosis (44.4% mAP@50) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (57.8% mAP@50).
A multi-stage evaluation methodology that balances performance metrics (mAP, Recall) with computational complexity is necessary to identify efficient solutions for practical clinical deployment.

Previous object detection benchmarks often failed to distinguish between the presence and absence of lesions due to the lack of healthy skin images in training datasets, a limitation addressed by this study's novel dataset design.
important_remarks: The study addresses a critical limitation in prior dermatological AI research by including a dedicated 'background' class of healthy skin images, which significantly improved Recall (from 66.7% to 71.6%) and mitigates false positives in real-world clinical settings.

Performance varies significantly by lesion type; the model excels on benign lesions (e.g., Pigmented Benign Keratosis mAP@50: 95.8%) but struggles with malignant lesions, particularly Actinic Keratosis (Recall: 48.5%, mAP@50: 44.4%) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (Recall: 51%).

The dataset is curated from the ISIC archive and augmented to balance class sizes (1000 images per class), but it relies on pre-labeled bounding boxes from licensed dermatologists, which may introduce annotation bias or limit generalizability to unannotated raw clinical data.

The YOLOv9c model was selected using a novel multi-stage scoring system that balances performance metrics (mAP, Recall) with computational complexity (parameter count < 30 million), prioritizing efficiency for potential deployment over raw accuracy alone.

Direct comparison with other studies is challenging due to variations in dataset composition, number of classes, and task definitions (detection vs. classification); the reported mAP@50 of 72.5% is competitive given the higher complexity of detecting 10 lesion classes plus background.

The study does not report external validation on independent datasets outside the curated ISIC-based split, limiting the assessment of generalizability to diverse populations or different imaging devices.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by a strict 80-10-10 train-validation-test split, but the use of augmentation (SafeRotate, ShiftScaleRotate) on specific classes (Dermatofibroma, Solar Lentigo) to balance the dataset may affect the independence of the test set if not carefully managed.
